chapters.title: The most misunderstood medical condition
chapters.text:
Depression and anxiety are some of those things that you can't exactly know how they feel until you experience them yourself. Being around a depressed person can only give you an idea of how they feel. You can't really know.

A depressed person usually looks healthy from the outside, so it's easy to think they aren't going through hell. In reality, being depressed is like being on fire from within, but that fire is invisible, so people can't understand its extent.

Only a depressed person knows what depression really feels like. The best you can do is try to empathize.

However, this summary will take you into the heart and mind of a depression survivor. By the end of this summary, you would have an intimate idea of how depression feels and what depressants battle all day long. Needless to say, this summary would increase your empathy for depressants and people dealing with anxiety. The moment you begin to understand the untold torments that depression unleashes on people, you would begin to see things on a whole new level, and no more join the rest of the world to say hurtful things to depressants such as, “Be strong and get over it. It's not the end of the world.” Instead, you'll start making statements like, “I don't know how you feel right now, but I just want you to know that it's okay to feel this way. I'm here for you.”

Words like those in the statement above make depressed people feel less weird, and more importantly, reassured of love and company.

If you're a depressed person, it's important to understand that you're not alone and that even if you can't see it, there is truly a light at the end of the tunnel. This summary is about a depression survivor. Matt didn't have it any different, and if he can make it out of that terrible blackhole called depression, so can you!

chapters.title: The mysterious nature of depression is sponsored mainly by ignorance
chapters.text:
Depression is terrible. It's horrific. It's the worst thing anyone can wish on their enemy. But that still doesn't make it an incurable illness. As a matter of fact, the treatment for depression is so available and affordable that no one should die from depression.

If this is the case, then why do statistics indicate that it results in more deaths than many other types of violence combined?

Here are a few reasons for this:

Ignorance. Ignorance is not bliss. It has been killing people since the dawn of humanity, and that's not going to change. 9 out of 10 people who suffer from depression don't even know it's depression until someone — a medical professional or a knowledgeable family member — tells them what it is. Far too many people have died from depression because they thought their experience was unique to them and refused to seek medical help. This happens everywhere, but especially in third-world nations.

Silence. Depression or no depression, silence is lethal. And mostly, it's societal conditioning that shuts people's mouths. A typical example is this: as per the World Health Organization (WHO), depression affects more women than men, yet the mortality rate due to depression is higher among men than women. That's startling if you think about it.

Logic demands that the number of women dying from depression and anxiety should exceed that of men since more women are affected, but the reverse is the case. Consider this UK suicide statistic shared by Matt Haig:

In 1981, there were 2,466 instances of women in the UK dying by suicide. Fast forward thirty years, and that number nearly halved to 1,391. In contrast, the figures for men were 4,129 in 1981 and increased to 4,590 thirty years later.

In the US, the ratio of male to female suicide is 4:1, and in Greece, it's 6:1. In only two regions — China and Hong Kong — the rate of suicide deaths is higher among women than men.

This certainly beats logic, but the reason is simple: men die in silence. Most men have been conditioned to believe that being open about mental illness is a sign of weakness — a feminine thing — so they man up and die in silence.

Depression begins to lose its grip on you the moment you start telling others about it.

Don't keep your pain to yourself. Be open and seek help from family, friends, and medical professionals. You will be glad you did.

Did you know? Depressants fear abandonment and loneliness more than anything. Studies show that depressed people are less likely to commit suicide when they have a good support system.

chapters.title: Having thin skin is not a bad thing
chapters.text:
Depressives often wish they had tougher skins. This reasoning seems to make sense when you consider it.

A tough skin will mean you feel less pain. The happenings around will not affect you so much, making it easier to deal with depression.

However, there's no scientific evidence to prove this. Love and care from the people that matter most to you are the strongest forces that can help you overcome depression, not tough skin.

Developing the tough skin mindset also suggests you want to hide your feelings from others. And that's not good for depression and anxiety. So, stop wishing for tougher skin.

What you should do instead is be grateful for the people around you who genuinely care for you. Accept their gestures of love and be open about how you're feeling each time. Sometimes, you may have just one person you can truly be open to, such as a significant other, a very close friend, or a parent. The number doesn't count. Knowing the person accepts your vulnerability is what's more important.

When it comes to human relationships, choose quality over quantity.

Having thin skin has its advantages. The fact that you can feel things easily means you're more positioned to absorb and enjoy life. One of our primary evolutionary qualities is the ability to feel and react to what we feel. Having thin skin means your feelings are heightened. When it comes to joy, for example, you easily pick and bask in it while others are still wondering what's happening. Of course, the downside is that you have to be careful because you'll also feel negative emotions to the same extent.

To other people, it sometimes seems like nothing at all. You are walking around with your head on fire and no one can see the flames. ~ Matt Haig

Did you know? Depression diminishes with age. The older you get, the more the symptoms decline. This process is, however, slow and almost unnoticeable.

chapters.title: Time is like wine, don't gulp it if you want to enjoy it
chapters.text:
Imagine the number of years you have to live is bottled up in a wine bottle. What's the best way to drink it if you really want to enjoy it? The answer is obvious. Drink it the same way you would your favorite wine.

No one gulps their favorite grape juice. What we do is pour it into a glass cup and sip it little by little, savoring every bit of it. That's how life should be lived.

But unfortunately, we don't approach life in the same way. We know that our time is limited, so we try to get things done as fast as possible. We can't wait to graduate high school, finish college, make money, get married, and on and on. But does speed slow down time?

It doesn't. Rushing your life won't make you have more than 24 hours in a day. It won't increase the number of days in a year either. Time is constant.

Don't get it wrong, though. It's important to set goals and work towards them. If you can achieve all the results you desire in a shorter time than others, go for it. However, don't do it at the expense of your life.

Don't sacrifice your life to get things that you later can't enjoy.

For every goal you set, achieving it isn't what's most important. More important than achievement is enjoying the process. Let's say your goal is to become the president of your country. As good as that is, you can't guarantee that you'll live long enough to actualize the dream. So which is better, enjoying the process towards becoming president or putting yourself through sufferings, hoping to enjoy only when you achieve the goal?

chapters.title: Stick to the remedy that works for you
chapters.text:
Depression is something that has been around for centuries, and billions of people have experienced it at some point in their lives.

Because it has been around for so long, different remedies have been developed to cure it. Such remedies include anything from traditional herbal medication to primitive therapy, religious practices, and modern medicine and therapy.

All of these have been proven to cure depression. However, the key point to understand is that depression is not a straightforward, one-dimensional illness. It's highly mysterious, unpredictable, and — more often than not — the experiences are unique to everyone.

Because of this, if you're suffering from depression for the first time, you may have somewhat of a trial and error experience in searching for a remedy that works for you. Don't panic if what is recommended to you doesn't seem to work, and don't get worried if something absurd works for you either.

If licking wallpapers is what keeps your depression and anxiety at bay, then heck, go for it!

Common remedies include pills, exercise, therapy, taking a walk once in a while, reading, and writing. Try any of these to see if your soul can find solace in them, but what you want to do is not get attached to traditional methods that aren’t working for you. Explore to find that solution that both relieves you and gives you a sense of hope, and it’s okay if what you cling to chances regularly.

Life is waiting for you. You might be stuck here for a while, but the world isn’t going anywhere. Hang on in there if you can. Life is always worth it. ~ Matt Haig

chapters.title: A friendly message to non-depressants
chapters.text:
Because depression is such a common thing, the chances are that your friend, spouse, kid, parent, or love interest might experience it at some point in their lives. Depression is tricky, so if you're not armed with sufficient information, you probably won't know how to deal with it.

Now, will show you how.

Let's start with how to identify depression.

As stated earlier, depression is an invisible experience, and due to naivety, the affected person won't easily know they are depressed. Here are a few warning signs to look out for:

• Feeling of unworthiness
• Fatigue
• Loss of appetite
• Irritability
• Slow movements and slow speech
• Anhedonia: The inability to derive pleasure from activities that are typically enjoyable or pleasurable.
• Sudden introversion

You'll earn more points in fighting depression if you're able to identify it early enough.

Of course, most of these symptoms could point to a natural experience or symptoms of a different disease. But all the same, if they persist for more than a week (especially when it's a combination of the above symptoms), then the person needs to visit the hospital.

Being there for a depressant is one of the hardest things anyone can do. Just as with any mental illness, you may find that the patient often says and does hurtful things to the person looking after them.

Try not to let it get into you. And be rest assured that those occasional outbursts aren't intentional. Understand that you're appreciated, even if they don't say it often.

More than 90% of depression survivors agree that their loved ones were the reason they made it through the horrific experience without killing themselves. Depressants suffer isolation anxiety so much that it's only the assured company of their dearest ones that keeps them from ending their lives.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Does depression just happen, or is it genetic? Are there warning signs that tell you someone will experience depression in 5, 10, or even 15 years? If so, are there proactive measures you can take to prevent depression?

These questions don't only exist in your mind. Scientists have battled with them for centuries. And to be honest, there isn't a satisfactory answer yet. Depression remains a mysterious subject.

However, there are some points to note:

• Your chances of experiencing depression, panic attacks, and anxiety are multiplied when someone in your bloodline has experienced the same things before.
• Most people who suffer depression in adulthood had panic attacks, anxiety, and other signs of depression scattered through their childhood.
• There is no proven scientific method for avoiding depression before it hits. If there is, it's not yet popular.
• People with no history of depression in their family can still experience it if life hits them hard in their weakest moments.

Early symptoms of depression include chronic dissatisfaction with life, hopelessness, fatigue, and sleeping disorders. Don't take these lightly when you observe them in yourself or someone you're close to. The best course of action is to speak to someone, preferably a trained professional. There's nothing to be shy about. Depression is more powerful when kept hidden. Telling someone about how you feel will help you conquer it easily.

Try this

Love doesn't take away all of life's troubles, nor does loving someone or being deeply loved take away your pains, but having a strong support system would help you go through depression — or other painful experiences — more easily. Here's some advice: don't joke with people who genuinely love you. They are your ultimate support system!

Read it till the end?
